mapping.a_element: N one-dimensional random walkers evolving stochastically
mapping.b_element: finite population of stochastically reproducing players

mapping.a_element: splitting probability of reaching one absorbing boundary before the other
mapping.b_element: probability of fixation on a pure strategy at the population boundary

brief_justification: Both are stochastic-dynamics papers, but the transferable machinery differs: paper A solves a first-passage/persistence problem for memory-coupled walkers via an exact representation of a shared trail field (persistence exponents, kth-arrival splitting probabilities), whereas paper B analyzes stochastic evolutionary game dynamics under demographic noise using timescale separation to explain long-lasting interior polymorphic (metastable coexistence) states. There is no role-for-role correspondence between an exactly-solvable trail-field first-passage calculation and a timescale-separated metastability analysis; the only overlap is the generic 'both are stochastic', and paper B's central object (interior polymorphism) is the opposite of reaching an absorbing boundary.
